title: The Name on the Ledger
special_element: The entry predates the character's arrival by a generation, in a hand that is not theirs — yet the name is spelled in the exact private form their family uses at home, the form no one outside the household has ever known.
summary: The Hollow Ward Post smells of old paper and something sweet, like candle-wax left too long in the sun.
body: The Hollow Ward Post smells of old paper and something sweet, like candle-wax left too long in the sun. The duty ledger lies open on the desk — entries in a dozen different hands, the oldest faded to rust-brown. Near the bottom, in ink that has not yet dried, is your name.

Special element: The entry predates the character's arrival by a generation, in a hand that is not theirs — yet the name is spelled in the exact private form their family uses at home, the form no one outside the household has ever known.
next_steps: **Stakes:** The ledger is not a list of who passed through — it is a list of who is *owed for*. Your name is in the debt column, entered a generation before you were born, in your family's private hand. A duty was promised in your name, and the post has kept your place. Now you have arrived, and the entry beside your name has begun to dry — as though someone is finally ready to collect.

**Next steps:** Find out what was promised, and by whom — a name in the signing column the family will not discuss. Decide whether to pay the duty, refuse it (the post does not take refusal well), or learn what the post actually is before you do either.

**Twists:** A second entry, directly below, reads *departed* — dated tomorrow. The signing hand in the column is one the character will recognise the first time they see a family document — it is their own, not yet written.

title: Ferryman's Fare
special_element: Old Marrow never wanted money. The coins go into the river — they always have — and what the ferryman truly collects is a **secret, spoken aloud on the crossing, so the water can hear it.** The PC paid in metal and never paid the real fare. Until they return to that bank, find that ferryman, and speak the secret owed, the coin keeps coming back — cold and waiting, wherever they sleep.
summary: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 1 · Mood: mysterious
body: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 1 · Mood: mysterious

You crossed a river once — grey morning, the water swollen and cold, a ferryman who had your name on his tongue before you gave it. You counted out the fare. He took it without looking, watched you reach the far bank in a voice like rope dragged over wet wood, and never once looked at the water.

That was a season ago. Last night, before dawn, someone left a single coin on your windowsill. It was cold to the touch, the way only river-water is cold. Your name was on one face — not printed, not stamped. Scratched in, like a reminder.

Special element: Old Marrow never wanted money. The coins go into the river — they always have — and what the ferryman truly collects is a **secret, spoken aloud on the crossing, so the water can hear it.** The PC paid in metal and never paid the real fare. Until they return to that bank, find that ferryman, and speak the secret owed, the coin keeps coming back — cold and waiting, wherever they sleep.
next_steps: **Stakes:** The debt is quiet for now — a coin, a chill. But it is a marker, and something on the far side of every crossing knows where the PC stands.
**Next steps:** Return to the crossing and name the secret. Or don't — and learn what the river does when a toll runs long overdue.
Marrow doesn't want a shameful secret. He wants the one true thing the PC has never said aloud to anyone — and saying it, to the river, changes something in them. The DM decides what.
The crossing the PC remembers may not be the one they took. Marrow ferried someone with their face the same morning.
If the PC has already met Marrow's Toll, they may recognise this coin in the river-tally — and understand, with sick clarity, what they're being counted toward.
